sentences.text: Right.
We were greeted by the women who gave us some food and put us on the boat, and I was assigned a cabin with a girl who was a few years older than I.
Total stranger.
And probably because she was older and she understood what was happening, she was weeping all night long, and I wept with her.
So that was really the first time I remember crying at all.
No, I don't believe so.
I certainly did, yeah.
Yeah.
OK.
Yes.
I have one-- I need to put my glasses on-- that-- that was published in an anthology.
It's called and it was compiled by Charles Fishman, edited by Charles Fishman.
And he reproduced this poem in it.
Should I read it?
It's called 'The Brooch.' 'White bone rose, silver thorns piercing my breast, fastening our past each to the other with questions never asked, answers never given.
Deep in the center, I see you still, mother, standing bravely, watching me go on a trainload of children, escaping.
I can never even get through this one.
I just--
I wrote that-- the white thorn rose is about a brooch that I-- a pin that I have.
And so I wrote this after my mother died, in 1967.
And between that time and the time when my husband and I first went back to Germany again in '72, I think-- '71 or '72-- is when I wrote that poem.
No.
I wish I had.
I-- all I have is some letters that my mother saved, and I have a few of those.
I arrived in Harwich, which is spelled H-A-R-W-I-C-H.
And it's a port city.
And from there, I'm put on a train with the rest of the kids to London.
I left on March the 3rd, 1939, so it would have been the 4th.
The weather was, it seems to me, typically English.
No sun, perhaps a little rain.
I  don't know.
Not very warm.
It was March.
And pretty dreary.
The blue dress?
Sure.
I had a little suitcase, and I had my number around my neck and I-- and this little beret on my head.
And-- and my brother was in London to meet me.
Yeah.
At-- at the London station, yeah.
Well, he took me with him to his room.
He was-- he was renting a room in a boarding house.
And his landlady was there too, and she was awfully nice.
Matter of fact, she had a dog called Spot, and now I could say the dog is under the table.
It wasn't wasted.
And she had a daughter, a little older than I.
They were-- it's-- I remember them being very kind to me, because I really didn't know English at all.
And I used those little dictionaries, I know.
He was working.
He was supposed to be-- he was sent to England originally to go to school because he couldn't finish his higher education in-- high-- his high school, even, in Germany.
And so he went to England to finish this up.
But by now, he was working.
He's-- he was some kind of technician.
He was also in the radio field.
No, I didn't.
I didn't stay with him at all.
He took me right over to the Liebermans and--
They were people-- the people that were taking me in, and the only Jewish family, as I said before.
And they were in a very poor section.
You had to go down the steps.
They had a basement apartment that they lived in.
And not too sanitary either, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were wonderful to-- to open their home to me and to take me in.
Why they did it, I don't know.
But I-- they must have felt it their-- their duty to do this, you know.
Unfortunately, it didn't work out because-- if I may, I'll go ahead with my-- with the story of the Liebermans.
They had also a daughter about my age, and she-- she was a little resentful of me, I suppose.
And we had to share a room.
Yeah, yeah, sure.
And to make matters worse, she had lice on her head, and I-- I caught them from her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which didn't-- which I wrote home about.
I didn't write it that way.
I said, my head itches.
And my mother asked an acquaintance that she had-- a business acquaintance in the neighborhood-- and-- to look in on me.
And this lady was horrified, I guess.
Yeah, yeah.
At the Lieberman home.
I mean, I was this spoiled little princess, you know, and I was coming into a situation like this.
She took me out of there.
No, not right away.
I mean, things didn't happen that quick.
But she came a couple of days later, I guess, and called and--
Drove me away.
She took me to her house, which was the exact opposite of this.
This was sumptuous, and to me it seemed like a palace.
And everything was beautiful, except she also-- this almost sounds like a fairy story, you know?
She also had a daughter, an only child, who also didn't like me.
I didn't like her either.
One only child, mm-hm.
I mean, I can certainly put myself in their places and say, hey, wait a minute, you know.
Who is this kid that has-- that I now have to share my bedroom with?
And so this-- this child, for instance, she had riding-- she was given riding lessons, and I wanted so much to be riding on a horse too.
This is-- 11 years old, that goes with the territory.
And the mother used to say, yeah, next time you'll  ride.
I never did.
She never kept that promise.
And I used to fight with this girl.
And at one point, I was-- we were scratching each other's eyes out,  and the mother said, I'm going to-- I have to send you-- if you keep this up, I have to send you back to Germany.
And I remember thinking, well, that's fine with me.
I-- I don't mind going back.
But then I thought, well, they went to all that trouble to send me out, and it must be important, the reason that I'm here.
And maybe they wouldn't like that so much.
I don't believe so.
I don't think so.
Well, yes, I knew [? some, ?] of course.
You see, since I can remember, since Hitler was there-- and I mean, '33, I was five years old.
That's when Hitler came into power.
So he would-- he had always been in my life.
I didn't know anything else.
And I knew that it wasn't good to be Jewish.
For me, it wasn't good.
For my parents, it wasn't good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that there had been incid-- I mean, there was Kristallnacht, for sure.
I knew all about that.
But that's--
Yeah, right.
But I don't believe that I put it into any kind of historical context or into a-- what will happen in the future.
I don't think that I thought that far.
No, I wasn't yet going to school.
And I guess it was the second family that arranged that for me.
And there will-- there will always be some blanks in my understanding of my own history.
But I landed up in a boarding school in Norwich.
From London?
Norwich from London, I'm not really sure.
Nothing's ever terribly far from anywhere in England.
Let's say another half hour, hour's train ride, maybe.
I think so.
I think so.
Whether she had any-- whether my parents had anything to do with it, I don't know.
But it was called the Lonsdale House School.
It was a boarding school for girls, and it was a private school.
And I think, perhaps, that I was a free boarder.
I think so.
I believe so.
Because I can't imagine who would have paid for it.
I was, yes.
We were writing back and forth to each other still.
Mine were very silly.
Oh, you know, I'm learning-- oh, in one-- In one letter, I said something like, I know the English word for parrot.
Do you?
And never explaining it, you know?
This is all in German, you-- you know.
Now I'm bilingual, yes.
Yeah.
My parents' letters were always, we're fine, and we'll come to you as soon as we can, and be a good girl.
That was very important.
Yeah.
At this point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, maybe half a year, not quite.
Actually, for me it was perfect.
It was-- number one, we all wore a uniform, and they found a uniform for me too.
And that was good because I didn't stick out like a sore thumb.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very disciplined.
It was Christian, and I was indoctrinated, shall we say, into the Christian religion at that point.
Of course.
No, no.
Not that I knew of, or teachers.
And-- and I just went right along with it, you know.
And we had scripture reading, and I even got a prize in religion.
Would you believe?
I still have it.
It's a King James version of the New Testament.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But it was fine for me, and I was doing well.
And the headmistress, whose name was Miss Callis, C-A-L-L-I-S, in case I'm not pronouncing it right, was very nice to me.

And at one point-- this was already after the war began-- she, I guess, had written to my parents, when she-- as she-- when she still could, to say that if anything should happen to them, she would become my legal guardian, which was really something.
She saw to it that, on holidays, when the children all-- when the girls went all home, I would have a place to go too.
So one time, I went with another girl.
Another time, I went with a teacher, who took me to her house for vacation.
I think so.
I think so.
Oh, yeah.
But-- but you have to realize that it was their mission in life, perhaps, to save my soul because I didn't, in their view, didn't have the right religion, you know.
So I was-- they  had set out to convert me, and they did a good job of it for a while.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
What was I going to say?
And I-- little by-- oh, and they taught me-- they taught me English in a wonderful way with a primer, as if I were just starting out school.
You know, I was now-- by now, I was 12.
They taught me the English money with car-- little cardboard cutouts, and things like that.
And it wasn't long.
I think it took maybe four, five weeks, and I was talking English.
What were some of the subjects that you were learning at school?
Pretty much the same as I had been studying in Germany.
The religion, of course, was big and heavy there.
But-- and-- no, I-- I don't think I had any problems.
I think I was-- at this point, I was still a good student.
At other people's homes, yeah.
Up until the war began, I was.
The war-- yeah, the war with England began when Hitler invaded Poland on September 1st, 1939.
Yeah.
And I had been in England since March.
So that's-- up until then, we could communicate still.
Yeah, I recall that.
I was-- I was at one of my friend's homes.
It was still the end of the summer vacations.
And that Sunday-- oh, I don't know if it was a Sunday.
Anyway, we were all-- we all went into the local church.
And my-- my own feelings were, yeah, I'll do everything for the war effort to-- to beat these Germans who kicked me out and, you know.
At that point, I was beginning to understand a little more.
Yes.
He was still in London.
All this time, he was in London and he was working.
And as a matter of fact, to save postage, because neither of us had much money-- he maybe a few pennies more than I-- I would send him my letters.
And then he would add it to his own letters and send them on to Germany.
But by the time the war began, this became increasingly difficult.
And the Red Cross allowed postcards to be sent with 25 words on them, I think in both directions.
I'm pretty sure but-- no, I think maybe just from Germany to England.
And the 25 words, that would take about two months to reach England.
How they got it  there in the first place is a marvel.
But 25 words-- all you can say and all anybody ever did say was, we're well, we'll see you soon.
You know.
Be good.
Well, only insofar as it changed everybody else's life in England.
We were issued our gas masks, and we were issued our food rationing cards.
And we-- we did drill-- air raid drills, and we had the black out.
The-- the war things that go on.
No.
Oh, no.
When I came back from this vacation and the war started is when Lonsdale House was no longer able to take boarders, and I guess they just took day students.
I'm-- I'm not sure if they did that right away.
There was even talk of closing the school.
Because Norwich is near the east coast, and the east coast is next-- is just across the water from Germany.
And the-- and there were-- there were bombing raids, and one thing and another.
And then there was talk of inva-- fear of invasion, a  lot of rumors about that.
And I still wonder-- we will wonder why Hitler didn't invade England, you know, but thank god he didn't.
And so I could no longer stay there as a boarder.
And Miss Callis, the headmistress, arranged for me to-- to live with her brother and his family in what's called the Midlands of England, in a-- in a little town called Wellingborough.
She took me there and-- by train.
We did everything by train or bus.
Very strict, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but kind, in their way.
I-- I was with them for about three and 1/2 years, it turned out, and thought of myself as their-- as their, not their daughter exactly, but part of their family.
[INAUDIBLE]
They had a daughter who had been going to my Lonsdale School, because that was her aunt's school, after all.
But she was older than I and she-- she left before I did,  and she-- she joined one of the armed services in England.
And they had a son.
They also took in a-- the son was in the navy.
They also took in a ref-- a-- an evacuee from London.
There were-- there was a different, a fine distinction, between an evacuee and a refugee.
I was the refugee.
She was the evacuee.
Well, the evacuee ca-- was sent out of London because of the bombing-- the Blitz that started pretty soon after, to go.
But her parents were still in London, and she could go back anytime she wanted to, and many did.
So their parents knew where they were.
They could visit them.
They could see each other.
I, as a refugee, had-- had to leave my country and learn a new language and not see my parents.
None at all.
A little later in the story, there was another girl in my class at-- at the next school that I was going to in Wellingborough who was a-- a German Jewish girl, and we became friends.
Yeah.
That's it, yeah.
This was a public school that I walked to back and front.
My children are always laughing at me because I said-- I told them that I had to walk uphill both ways.
But it was true, and when I took my husband later on to show him, he had to agree with me.
You walked down the hill and up the hill, and then you walked down the hill and up the hill again so-- [LAUGHS] The schooling was different, of course.
It was high school by now.
And I was learning French.
No, they were trying to teach me French.
And Latin.
Oh, yeah, I think we had Latin in-- in the boarding school as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a little more serious.
Different teachers for different subjects.
And again, all girls.
The schools in England were se-- segregated.
My schooling went downhill.
It was-- I don't know what happened.
Partly because, perhaps, there wasn't anybody around to really help me with my studies anymore, and it felt like nobody else was really interested.
This may not be true, this is-- but this is how I perceived it.
And I lost interest.
So I did quite poorly, except in music and art and the-- the subjects that I liked, and probably English.
What kind of a kid was I?
I was a s-- well, I had friends, but I-- I liked being alone.
I was always-- I always thought I was different.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to  tell  you the truth, to this day, I think, there's part of me that is different.
And that's not necessarily a good thing.
I-- I sometimes would like to be not so different, but there you are.
I was always thin and short, and I didn't think I was particularly beautiful.
I never will be.
But I didn't have a very good image, coming to think of it.
Only the letters that I wrote.
I don't know why I didn't.
I wish I could sometimes turn the clock back just for that.
Wouldn't really, though.
I had-- I enjoyed my music lessons a lot.
I was learning the piano.
I had taken cello in-- lessons in Germany, and that stopped after two years.
And then in England, I got piano lessons, and I did enjoy that a lot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- and I was very-- I was becoming extremely religious and rather evan-- evangelical.
And I remember trying to convert my brother, or-- or at least to, you know, open something up with my brother, and  he got very concerned.
I don't know.
He didn't really react to me at all.
What I-- he might have written something to my parents, but I'm not sure.
Well, they were trying to get out of Germany seriously now.
In fact, my father had been released from Dachau on the promise to start arranging his rel-- his leaving Germany.
And having said that, they also didn't make it easy for him to leave.
And his-- his bank accounts were locked up, and he couldn't get to them.
The business was sold for a pittance.
The-- the money wasn't there anymore.
Plus, he couldn't get a visa to go anywhere.
No-- there wasn't a country in the world that wanted him.
Yes.
My mother could have gone to England as a domestic, this woman who had never done a stitch of work in her life.
But because my father didn't have any manual skills, and she refused to leave him-- leave without him.
So England was already out.
Yes, there were-- there was a cousin that emigrated to Argentina, but he was a refugee himself.
Another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, an-- an-- her brother escaped to Holland.
I'm not sure if she knew-- at what point she knew that he was in Holland.
And--
No, this was my mother's brother, another uncle.
The-- the uncle that lived next door to me got to America before my parents did and was-- and I have letters.
I have documents in the house.
And I was astounded, though, when I read them just recently, how much work he put into getting them here.
It wasn't easy.
And they had to have a guarantor, is it called?
So he found this very distant relative who lived here, and he became the guarantor.
Then they got a visa.
Then the visa fell through.
And they got passage on a ship, finally, leaving from Portugal.
And a rather similar story to my husband's, who also left via Spain and Portugal.
Well, my parents did too, only they had to take a train to Spain.
And they were supposed to go on one ship, and then that ship-- somehow that didn't work out.
That-- to make a long story short, the third ship was the-- finally the one that they were able to get onto.
But this was in July, 1941.
Very late, not-- I really-- and the day after they left Stuttgart, the American consulate closed down.
So they literally escaped by one day.
I wasn't aware of it, no.
Yeah.
Oh, I would-- well, depending on if it was wintertime, I would have to get up and sort of quick wash myself in the-- from a bowl that had probably a layer of ice on top, and then get dressed.
And very often, I would get dressed in bed because it was so cold.
And-- well, and I went to school, and we had air raid drills, though.
We had to be ready to jump out of bed in the middle of the night.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But other than that, just a regular kid's day, I think.
Going to school, doing homework.
